tips.title: Tips & Guides

articles.title: Hydration Basics for Water Fasting
articles.body: Water fasting is not just about abstaining from food—it's about understanding how to stay hydrated. During a water fast, your body relies entirely on water and electrolytes to maintain optimal function. Drink water consistently throughout the day, aiming for at least 2-3 liters. Pure water is fine, but adding mineral salts or electrolyte supplements can help prevent dehydration and maintain energy levels. Listen to your body's signals: thirst, dry mouth, and fatigue may indicate you need to adjust your hydration strategy. Remember, quality of water matters too—filtered water is ideal to avoid unnecessary minerals or contaminants.

articles.title: Electrolytes and Timing
articles.body: Electrolytes—sodium, potassium, and magnesium—are crucial during extended water fasts. When you stop eating, your body excretes more electrolytes, which can lead to imbalances if not replenished. Common symptoms of electrolyte deficiency include muscle cramps, dizziness, and headaches. Consider taking a quality electrolyte supplement or consuming salt water (a pinch of sea salt in water) to maintain balance. The timing matters: spread electrolyte intake evenly throughout the day rather than taking it all at once. Most people benefit from 1,000-2,000mg of sodium daily during a fast. Always consult your doctor if you have cardiovascular concerns or take medications that affect electrolyte levels.

articles.title: Breaking a Fast Safely
articles.body: Breaking your fast correctly is just as important as the fast itself. Your digestive system needs time to reactivate after an extended period without food. Start with easily digestible foods: bone broth, vegetable soups, smoothies, or light salads. Avoid heavy, greasy, or sugary foods immediately after fasting—they can cause digestive distress and rapid blood sugar spikes. Eat slowly and chew thoroughly to aid digestion. For fasts longer than 24 hours, introduce solid foods gradually over 2-3 meals. Stay hydrated and monitor how your body responds to different foods. Many people find that eating nutrient-dense, whole foods helps them feel better and sustains the benefits of their fast.

articles.title: The Science Behind Ketosis
articles.body: Ketosis is a metabolic state your body enters during extended fasting, typically after 12-16 hours without food. In this state, your liver converts fat into ketones, which become your primary fuel source instead of glucose. This shift has profound effects: increased mental clarity, reduced inflammation, and accelerated fat loss. Your body doesn't just use stored fat from fasting—ketosis also activates autophagy, a cellular cleaning process that removes damaged components. Most people can gauge they're in ketosis by sensing increased energy, reduced hunger, and a slightly metallic taste in their mouth. Drinking water and maintaining electrolytes helps optimize ketosis. Different people enter ketosis at different rates, depending on activity level, diet quality, and metabolic factors.

articles.title: Common Fasting Mistakes to Avoid
articles.body: Many people make common mistakes on their first fasts that undermine their results or make them uncomfortable. The biggest mistake: not consuming enough electrolytes, leading to headaches and fatigue. Second: breaking a fast too aggressively with heavy foods, causing digestive issues. Third: skipping meals leading up to a fast without proper preparation—easing into a fast with lighter meals beforehand helps tremendously. Fourth: ignoring hunger signals entirely—some hunger is normal, but extreme hunger may mean you need electrolytes or should shorten your next fast. Fifth: fasting too frequently without recovery days. Your body needs time to adapt. Start with 16-hour fasts and gradually work toward longer durations. Track how you feel and adjust your approach based on your unique response.

profile.title: My Profile
profile.logAtLeastTwoWeights: Log at least 2 weights to see your progress chart.
profile.driveUnconfiguredBody: Google Drive sync needs an OAuth client ID to be added to app.json. Use the local backup / restore below in the meantime.
profile.driveBackupDoneBody: Your data is safely stored in your Google Drive appData folder.
profile.driveEmptyBody: No WaterFastBuddy backup was found in your Drive. Run Backup first from another device.
profile.driveFailedBody: Couldn't reach Google Drive. Check your connection and try again.
profile.driveSignedOutBody: You're signed out of Google Drive. The next backup will ask to sign in again.
profile.restoreHelp: Paste a backup JSON produced by Share backup. Use this to move your data manually (e.g. save the JSON to Drive or email, then paste it back on a new device).
profile.restoreInvalidBody: That doesn't look like a WaterFastBuddy backup.
profile.restoreDoneBody: Your data has been imported successfully.
profile.restoreFailedBody: Something went wrong while importing your data.
